questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: which access order defines a stack?

answers.text: The way I understand it: push and pop normally operate at the same end.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Push and pop normally operate at the same end.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Push and pop normally operate at the same end.

answers.text: Short version: Last in, first out.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Last in, first out.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Last in, first out.

answers.text: I'd go with this: First in, first out describes a queue.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. First in, first out describes a queue.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. First in, first out describes a queue.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: which access order defines a basic queue?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: First in, first out.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. First in, first out.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. First in, first out.

answers.text: The way I understand it: the newest item always leaves first.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A queue removes the oldest enqueued item first.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A queue removes the oldest enqueued item first.

answers.text: Short version: Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends.

questions.prompt: Forum check: what prerequisite does ordinary binary search need?

answers.text: From what I've seen, the target must be present.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Binary search also terminates correctly when the target is absent.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Binary search also terminates correctly when the target is absent.

answers.text: The practical answer is: binary search always runs in O(log n) time on a linked list.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Finding the middle of a basic linked list is not constant time, so the usual array bound does not apply.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Finding the middle of a basic linked list is not constant time, so the usual array bound does not apply.

answers.text: My answer: The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used.

questions.prompt: I've seen mixed takes, so: is hash-table lookup always O(1)?

answers.text: I think the important part is this: resizing strategy affects amortized insertion cost.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Resizing strategy affects amortized insertion cost.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Resizing strategy affects amortized insertion cost.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, collisions can degrade worst-case lookup, potentially to linear time.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Collisions can degrade worst-case lookup, potentially to linear time.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Collisions can degrade worst-case lookup, potentially to linear time.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Expected lookup can be O(1) with a suitable hash function and load factor.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Expected lookup can be O(1) with a suitable hash function and load factor.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Expected lookup can be O(1) with a suitable hash function and load factor.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: when does breadth-first search find shortest paths?

answers.text: My answer: It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: It directly handles arbitrary negative edge weights.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Weighted shortest paths require an algorithm suited to those weights.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Weighted shortest paths require an algorithm suited to those weights.

answers.text: The way I understand it: it explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what does stability mean for a sorting algorithm?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: The algorithm never uses extra memory.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Stability and memory usage are separate properties.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Stability and memory usage are separate properties.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Every implementation of quicksort is stable.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Quicksort is not inherently stable; stability depends on the algorithm and implementation.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Quicksort is not inherently stable; stability depends on the algorithm and implementation.

answers.text: From what I've seen, equal-key items keep their original relative order.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Equal-key items keep their original relative order.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Equal-key items keep their original relative order.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what does a binary min-heap guarantee?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: The minimum element is at the root.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The minimum element is at the root.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The minimum element is at the root.

answers.text: From what I've seen, every level is globally sorted from left to right.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A heap only guarantees the parent-child order property.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A heap only guarantees the parent-child order property.

questions.prompt: No searching: what problem does a disjoint-set union structure solve?

answers.text: This is what trips people up: It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: It returns the shortest weighted path between two vertices.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Union-find tracks connectivity, not path length.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Union-find tracks connectivity, not path length.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly constant amortized time.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly constant amortized time.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly constant amortized time.

questions.prompt: No searching: when is dynamic programming a useful approach?

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Whenever recursion appears, regardless of repeated work.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Recursion alone does not imply that dynamic programming is helpful.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Recursion alone does not imply that dynamic programming is helpful.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Dynamic programming always chooses the locally best option at each step.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Choosing a locally best option describes a greedy strategy, not dynamic programming in general.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Choosing a locally best option describes a greedy strategy, not dynamic programming in general.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: can standard Dijkstra's algorithm handle negative edge weights?

answers.text: My answer: Dijkstra is correct with nonnegative edge weights.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Dijkstra is correct with nonnegative edge weights.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Dijkstra is correct with nonnegative edge weights.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Yes, as long as there is no negative cycle.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A negative edge can invalidate Dijkstra's greedy finalization step.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A negative edge can invalidate Dijkstra's greedy finalization step.

answers.text: The way I understand it: bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed.

questions.prompt: Curious where people land on this: when does a topological ordering exist?

answers.text: My answer: For a directed acyclic graph.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. For a directed acyclic graph.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. For a directed acyclic graph.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: For every directed graph.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A directed cycle makes a topological ordering impossible.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A directed cycle makes a topological ordering impossible.

answers.text: The way I understand it: a graph can have more than one valid topological ordering.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A graph can have more than one valid topological ordering.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A graph can have more than one valid topological ordering.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: what do red-black tree invariants provide?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, they force the tree to be perfectly balanced.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Red-black trees allow controlled imbalance.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Red-black trees allow controlled imbalance.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Every root-to-leaf path has exactly the same total number of nodes.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Red-black invariants equalize black height, not the total node count of every path.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Red-black invariants equalize black height, not the total node count of every path.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Red-black tree lookup takes O(1) time in the worst case.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The height is logarithmic, so lookup is O(log n) in the worst case.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The height is logarithmic, so lookup is O(log n) in the worst case.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures
